title: Spine capping and foredge trimming
subtitle: Finishing the head, tail, and foredge of a sewn text block
excerpt: A freshly sewn text block has slightly uneven edges. Capping the spine ends and trimming the foredge gives the book block a clean, professional finish before casing in. The tools are a craft knife, metal ruler, and a block of scrap wood for support.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on cutting and finishing the text block. The method of trimming text blocks with a plough or knife is standard in the bookbinding tradition. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: The outer edge of a book opposite the spine. Also called the front edge or fore-edge (the hyphened form is traditional). The foredge is the first edge the reader handles when opening the book.
The amount by which the cover boards extend beyond the text block at the head, tail, and foredge. Standard squares are 3 mm on each side for most book formats.

content.text: After sewing and gluing up the spine, a text block typically has a slightly uneven
and ragged head and tail. Trimming before casing in gives the finished book a clean, even edge on all three open sides. The trimmed dimensions also set the

: the small amount by which the cover boards will extend past the text block. Traditional binderies used a plough and press for this work; at home, a craft knife and metal ruler on a firm surface does the job for short runs.

attrs.title: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades. Change the blade in the craft knife before starting: trimming a full text block dulls blades quickly, and a dull blade skids. Always cut against the metal ruler, keeping the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths back from the blade path.

items.name: Replacement blades, No. 11 (slug: craft-knife-blades-11)
items.description: Have several to hand. Change after every 8-10 cuts through a full-height text block.

items.name: Metal ruler, 60 cm (slug: metal-ruler-60cm)
items.description: The long ruler sits flat across the full height of the text block without rocking.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For aligning the text block edges before clamping.

items.name: Scrap wood block or book press
items.description: To hold the text block stable under even pressure during trimming. Two boards of scrap wood and several clamps serve well.

content.text: Allow the glued spine to dry fully before trimming, at least 4 hours, or overnight. A damp spine lets the block flex during cutting and the trimmed edge will be uneven.

Tap the foredge on a flat surface to roughly align the pages. Then tap the head edge. Place the block between two boards or in the press so the foredge projects about 5 mm beyond the boards. Clamp firmly.

Place the metal ruler against the foredge at the depth you want to trim. Make the first pass with only light pressure. You are scoring a line through the top few pages, not cutting the full depth in one pass. Make successive passes, increasing pressure, until the knife passes cleanly through the full stack.

Do not lift and re-position the ruler between passes. The ruler must stay in exactly the same position through all passes or the edge will show a step. Clamp the ruler at each end with your non-cutting hand, with thumb behind the cut line and fingers pressing down.

Rotate the block 90 degrees in the press so the head edge is accessible. Clamp. Align the ruler and trim in the same way. For the tail, flip the block end-for-end and repeat. On a well-sewn text block, the head and tail need very little trimming, a single pass is often enough to level any minor unevenness.

items.symptom: Cut edge shows a slight curve rather than a straight line
items.cause: Block shifted during cutting, or not clamped firmly enough
items.fix: Re-clamp the block more firmly. If the error is small, a second pass with the ruler positioned to remove the high point corrects it.

items.symptom: Blade skips across the paper surface rather than cutting
items.cause: Blade dull, or grain of the paper running against the cut direction
items.fix: Change the blade immediately. On subsequent passes, use a slower stroke with more pressure rather than trying to speed through.

items.symptom: Head or tail edge is not square to the spine
items.cause: Text block not pressed square before clamping
items.fix: The error will be hidden once the book is cased in and the boards added. For the next block, use the bone folder to square the block before pressing.
